messages.content: Add an FAQ section with 4 common questions people ask before starting a vending machine business.

context.title: Coffee vending for offices
context.metaDescription: Coffee vending machines for modern workplaces.

input.title: Common questions before starting a vending business
input.body: Starting a vending machine business involves several operational and logistical considerations. These frequently asked questions cover the essentials for prospective operators looking to build a sustainable route.

faqItems.question: How do I find locations for my machines?
faqItems.answer: Securing high-traffic locations is the most critical factor for success. Common spots include office buildings, warehouses, gyms, and medical centres. Most operators negotiate a small commission or a fixed monthly space rental fee with the property owner in exchange for providing the service.

faqItems.question: Is a vending machine business actually profitable?
faqItems.answer: Profitability depends on the machine's location, product mix, and maintenance costs. While individual transactions are small, a well-placed machine with popular snack or drink options can generate consistent passive income. Successful operators focus on scaling to multiple machines to cover overhead and increase total margins.

faqItems.question: What kind of utilities and space do I need?
faqItems.answer: Modern machines typically require a standard power outlet and, if you are installing a coffee or fresh food unit, a dedicated water line. You should also consider the floor space and weight-bearing capacity of the site, as fully stocked machines are heavy and need stable, level ground.

faqItems.question: How often will I need to restock and service machines?
faqItems.answer: The frequency of restocking depends entirely on sales volume. High-traffic locations may require servicing two or three times a week, while quieter offices might only need a weekly visit. Tracking inventory via remote monitoring software can help you plan your routes efficiently and ensure machines never sit empty.
